_comment: Ophis agent-wallet policy pack for Turnkey. Parameterized template: substitute the {{PLACEHOLDER}} values from ../addresses.json for your target chain, then create these as separate Turnkey policies on the sub-organization that holds the agent's key. Turnkey is deny-by-default and EFFECT_DENY overrides EFFECT_ALLOW, so this pack ALLOWS exactly two paths (the one-time approve and Ophis order signing) and adds two DENY guards. Attach it to a DEDICATED NON-ROOT API user for the agent; keep root credentials offline with your guardian. Schema: https://docs.turnkey.com/concepts/policies/language
_casing_warning: Turnkey compares addresses as case-sensitive strings. The EIP-712 docs mandate LOWERCASE hex for eth.eip_712.* conditions (uppercase may be rejected), while the eth.tx.* examples use checksummed hex. This template uses _LOWERCASE placeholders for eip_712 conditions and checksummed placeholders for eth.tx conditions. Verify the exact casing Turnkey applies to each field against a real signed payload before you rely on it.
_abi_note: eth.tx.contract_call_args['spender'] requires you to upload the token contract ABI to Turnkey so approve() is decoded. The arg key equals the ABI parameter name: canonical ERC-20 is approve(address spender,uint256 value) -> 'spender'/'value', but some tokens (e.g. WETH) name them 'guy'/'wad'. Confirm the names in the ABI you upload per token.
_allowlist_operator_note: The token allowlist below uses `field in ['{{TOKEN_ALLOWLIST}}']`. Some Turnkey policy-language references document `in` for integer/enum fields and recommend explicit equality with `||` for STRING fields such as addresses. Verify `in` on an address field against your Turnkey version before you rely on it; if it is not accepted, expand the allowlist to an OR chain instead, e.g. `(eth.eip_712.message['sellToken'] == '0xTOKEN_A' || eth.eip_712.message['sellToken'] == '0xTOKEN_B')` (and the same for buyToken and for eth.tx.to on the approve rule). A rejected condition fails closed (the agent cannot sign), so this is a liveness check, not a safety hole.

policies.policyName: Ophis {{CHAIN_NAME}}: allow approve() only to the vault relayer, only for allowlisted tokens
policies.condition: activity.type == 'ACTIVITY_TYPE_SIGN_TRANSACTION_V2' && eth.tx.chain_id == {{CHAIN_ID}} && eth.tx.value == 0 && eth.tx.function_name == 'approve' && eth.tx.contract_call_args['spender'] == '{{VAULT_RELAYER}}' && eth.tx.to in ['{{TOKEN_ALLOWLIST}}']
policies.notes: Optional bounded allowance: append  && eth.tx.contract_call_args['value'] <= {{MAX_APPROVE_AMOUNT_DECIMAL}}  (uint256 decimal, no quotes). Requires the token ABI uploaded so 'spender'/'value' decode. The eth.tx.* examples in Turnkey's docs use checksummed addresses; verify casing against a real payload.

policies.policyName: Ophis {{CHAIN_NAME}}: allow signing an Ophis order only (domain + receiver + token set pinned)
policies.condition: activity.type == 'ACTIVITY_TYPE_SIGN_RAW_PAYLOAD_V2' && activity.params.encoding == 'PAYLOAD_ENCODING_EIP712' && eth.eip_712.primary_type == 'Order' && eth.eip_712.domain.name == 'Gnosis Protocol' && eth.eip_712.domain.version == 'v2' && eth.eip_712.domain.chain_id == {{CHAIN_ID}} && eth.eip_712.domain.verifying_contract == '{{VERIFYING_CONTRACT_LOWERCASE}}' && eth.eip_712.message['receiver'] == '{{AGENT_WALLET_ADDRESS_LOWERCASE}}' && eth.eip_712.message['sellToken'] in ['{{TOKEN_ALLOWLIST_LOWERCASE}}'] && eth.eip_712.message['buyToken'] in ['{{TOKEN_ALLOWLIST_LOWERCASE}}']
policies.notes: verifying_contract is the Ophis settlement for this chain. On the 10 non-sovereign chains it equals the canonical GPv2 address (shared with CoW Swap); only Optimism and Unichain carry an Ophis-exclusive settlement. Addresses here MUST be lowercase per Turnkey's EIP-712 casing rule.

policies.policyName: Ophis {{CHAIN_NAME}}: deny the pre-hashed NO_OP bypass of the order field checks
policies.notes: MANDATORY companion. The eth.eip_712.* selectors are only populated when the caller submits structured typed data (PAYLOAD_ENCODING_EIP712). If the agent instead submits a pre-computed 32-byte digest with HASH_FUNCTION_NO_OP, Turnkey sees an opaque hash and the receiver/domain pins above cannot apply. This DENY closes that path. See https://www.turnkey.com/blog/hyperliquid-secure-eip-712-signing

policies.policyName: Ophis {{CHAIN_NAME}}: deny key/seed export by the agent user
policies.notes: Deny-by-default already blocks export for a non-root user; this makes it explicit. Only the offline guardian (root quorum) should ever export or migrate the key.
